Title: AMMF: Attention-Based Fusion for Cross-Architecture Binary Vulnerability
Abstract: Binary vulnerability detection remains a critical challenge in modern software security, especially in ecosystems like Go where cross-compilation enables the same codebase to run across diverse architectures. Variations introduced by different compilers, architectures, and optimization settings significantly complicate reliable vulnerability identification at scale.  This work presents AMMF, an attention-driven multi-feature fusion approach for cross-architecture binary vulnerability detection. The method integrates assembly-level semantic information with structural attributes extracted at both basic block and function levels. Instruction semantics are learned through embedding representations, while contextual relationships are modeled using a gated recurrent unit enhanced with self-attention. These semantic representations are fused with control-flow and function-level features via an attention-augmented convolutional architecture to produce unified and discriminative function embeddings.  AMMF adopts a two-stage detection strategy. First, a deep neural similarity model identifies candidate vulnerable functions from large-scale binary corpora. Second, control-flow graph matching refines these candidates to determine final vulnerabilities. This design improves both detection accuracy and efficiency while reducing false positives.  Experimental results show strong accuracy and recall across cross-architecture, cross-compiler, and cross-optimization scenarios. By combining semantic understanding with structural features under attention mechanisms, AMMF improves robustness in real-world settings, making it well-suited for securing software systems built with modern, cross-platform languages like Go.

Title: Real-Time ML Training Profiling in Go: Cut Costs by 50%
Abstract: Machine learning training workloads now consume staggering resources, with training GPT-3 alone requiring 1,287 MWh of energy. Yet production GPU utilization averages just 50-60%, meaning organizations routinely waste 60-70% of their cloud compute budgets. Without real-time visibility, engineers only discover bottlenecks after hours of wasted spend. This talk shares lessons learned building a multi-layer profiling system in Go that captures metrics across application, system, and infrastructure layers with as little as 2-5% runtime overhead. We cover the architecture and design choices made, how they played out in production, and where Go's concurrency primitives made the implementation practical at scale. The session walks through the three most costly performance failure modes we encountered: data pipeline bottlenecks (responsible for 40-50% of GPU underutilization cases), communication overhead in distributed training (accounting for 20-45% of total training time at scale), and straggler workers in heterogeneous environments (causing 30-40% of compute time to be wasted waiting for slower workers). For each, we explain what we tried, what worked, and what we would do differently. Beyond detection, the talk covers an automated recommendation layer that achieved 85% accuracy in root-cause identification and delivered an average 32% throughput improvement without requiring deep performance engineering expertise. The concrete outcomes: training costs reduced by 30-50%, energy consumption cut by 40-60%, and total training time reduced by 20-35% while maintaining convergence quality.

Title: Responsible Agentic AI for Equitable and Sustainable Telecom Networks
Abstract: Artificial intelligence is reshaping telecommunications infrastructure across 5G and emerging 6G ecosystems. In modern networks, intelligent services manage spectrum allocation, fault detection, traffic forecasting, and reinforcement learning based power optimization. These agent driven systems increase efficiency and adaptability, yet their design choices directly influence equity, sustainability, and accountability at scale.  Today, 4.0 billion people have high speed connectivity, while 1.5 billion remain under connected and 1.1 billion remain fully unconnected. AI driven optimization can expand coverage, but when resource allocation models are trained on historically uneven investment data, they risk reinforcing disparities by prioritizing high revenue urban regions over rural and economically disadvantaged communities. Without fairness constraints and measurable oversight, automated decision systems may entrench digital inequality rather than reduce it.  Environmental considerations add another layer of complexity. Reinforcement learning can reduce idle base station energy use by dynamically adjusting transmission power, and digital twin simulations help test efficiency strategies before deployment. At the same time, large scale model training and continuous inference require significant computational resources. The overall sustainability impact depends on whether operational gains outweigh AI’s infrastructure footprint.  This session presents a governance aligned approach to agentic AI in telecommunications, embedding transparency, fairness evaluation, responsibility mapping, and carbon reporting into network optimization workflows. By integrating these controls into intelligent systems rather than treating them as afterthoughts, operators can ensure that autonomous networks advance performance while delivering measurable societal and environmental benefit.

JobTitle1: Senior Software Development Engineer in Test (SDET)
Title: Building High-Performance Claims Systems with AI and EDI Pipelines
Abstract: Payer organizations are facing increasing claim complexity, rising administrative burden, and the need for high-throughput, reliable processing systems. This session explores how modern backend architectures can support AI-driven claims processing using machine learning, automation, and intelligent EDI pipelines.  Attendees will learn how machine learning models enhance claim classification and enable advanced anomaly detection beyond traditional rule-based systems. The session highlights how these models identify coding inconsistencies, routing issues, and high-risk scenarios earlier in the workflow, reducing reliance on manual reviews.  The discussion focuses on designing scalable systems that automate high-volume tasks such as data validation, eligibility checks, and claim routing. It also covers how EDI platforms process large-scale X12 transactions through parsing, enrichment, mapping, and compliance validation, supporting efficient data flow across services.  Emphasis is placed on building high-performance, concurrent processing pipelines that integrate AI models with existing systems. Real-world examples will illustrate how these architectures improve throughput, reduce errors, and maintain consistency under heavy workloads.  Participants will gain a practical blueprint for designing scalable, efficient backend systems that support AI-driven workflows, along with key considerations for integration, performance, and operational reliability.

Title: Architecting Intent-Driven AI Orchestration with Go
Abstract: As enterprises scale generative AI initiatives, they often face fragmentation—multiple disconnected AI services, duplicated capabilities, inconsistent user experiences, and growing operational complexity. This talk explores how to architect an intent-driven orchestration layer in Go to unify enterprise AI systems into a cohesive assistant ecosystem.  We’ll examine how Go’s concurrency model, strong typing, and performance characteristics make it well-suited for building orchestration services that coordinate LLMs, tools, and domain systems. At the core is an enterprise agentic AI platform that provides a single interface while abstracting complexity behind modular services.  Key components include intent analyzers for interpreting user requests, orchestrators for intelligent task routing, reasoning workflows for multi-step execution, and integration layers for vector-based retrieval systems and dynamic prompt libraries. We’ll discuss how function calling, prompt chaining, and state tracking can be implemented within Go services to move from passive Q&A to active, task-oriented workflows.  Through real-world enterprise scenarios across finance, HR, and IT, we’ll explore how Go-based orchestration enables reliable multi-step execution while maintaining governance, compliance, and operational control. Practical guidance will cover phased rollouts, cross-system integration patterns, and designing composable architectures that scale.  Attendees will leave with architectural patterns and implementation insights for building resilient, intent-driven AI systems in Go—transforming isolated AI tools into harmonized, production-ready ecosystems.

Title: AI Powered Automation for Scalable Enterprise Development in Go
Abstract: Enterprise web systems built with microservices, cloud native platforms, and API driven architectures have grown increasingly complex. Many organizations now manage hundreds of services, large API ecosystems, and distributed CI/CD pipelines across multiple environments. While these architectures improve scalability and speed of delivery, they also introduce significant cognitive load for engineering teams. Developers must understand service dependencies, internal standards, documentation repositories, and evolving system designs while maintaining productivity.  This talk explores how artificial intelligence is emerging as a practical productivity accelerator in enterprise development workflows, particularly in modern Go based service ecosystems. The session begins by examining AI powered knowledge intelligence systems that unify fragmented documentation across code repositories, architecture diagrams, ticketing systems, and internal knowledge bases. Through semantic search and retrieval augmented generation, developers can query institutional knowledge conversationally and quickly retrieve relevant architectural context.  The presentation then discusses AI assisted development environments that support engineers by generating service scaffolding, validating API contracts, suggesting refactoring improvements, and identifying potential security risks. These tools help automate repetitive tasks such as boilerplate generation, syntax correction, and dependency analysis, allowing engineers to focus on system design, reliability, and performance.  Finally, the session highlights AI assisted testing and onboarding workflows. Machine learning driven QA systems can analyze code paths and historical defect patterns to generate intelligent test scenarios, while AI guided onboarding assistants help new engineers understand complex service architectures.  The talk demonstrates how structured human and AI collaboration can improve developer productivity while maintaining engineering oversight in modern enterprise systems built with Go.

Title: Building Secure CI/CD Pipelines with Go-Based Automation
Abstract: Modern platform engineering teams are increasingly using Go to build fast, reliable, and cloud-native automation tools for CI/CD and infrastructure management.  In this talk, I explore how Go can be used to design secure, scalable CI/CD automation components that support modern DevOps pipelines. Drawing from real-world architecture experience across AWS, Azure, and GCP, I demonstrate how Go-based tooling can improve deployment reliability, enforce governance policies, and reduce operational risk.  We will focus on practical engineering patterns rather than theory, including how Go services can be integrated into CI/CD workflows, how to design for observability and resilience, and how to structure automation systems that scale across multi-cloud environments.  Attendees will gain insights into:  Why Go is well-suited for platform engineering and CI/CD automation How to design secure pipeline automation services using Go Common pitfalls when building infrastructure tooling Architecture patterns for scalable DevOps systems  This is a practical, experience-driven session aimed at engineers building real-world delivery platforms.

Title: Digital PMOs in Retail with Jira and Confluence: Governance Through Transparency
Abstract: Retail technology portfolios that span e commerce, supply chain, and in store platforms often struggle under traditional PMO models. Status tracking is frequently fragmented across spreadsheets and disconnected tools, making it difficult to maintain a single source of truth, identify dependencies early, or apply consistent governance controls. These gaps slow decision making, reduce transparency, and create operational inefficiencies across the portfolio.  This session presents a Digital PMO framework that integrates Jira and Confluence to establish a unified governance and execution environment. The framework translates established PMO and IT governance concepts into a practical operating model built on structured workflows, standardized templates, and integrated documentation. It connects strategic themes, initiatives, OKRs, epics, delivery telemetry, risk signals, and executive reporting within a single system of record that supports both engineering teams and leadership.  A retail case study demonstrates the framework’s application within an Automotive Care Center technology portfolio that involved more than 20 cross functional teams and approximately 50 initiatives per quarter over a six month period. Quantitative results included a 30 percent reduction in PMO operational effort within six months and an additional 30 percent reduction at 12 months, while program manager roles were reduced from eight to three. Stakeholders also reported improved transparency, faster trade off decisions, and greater trust in portfolio reporting.  Attendees will learn how integrated Jira and Confluence environments can support scalable governance, real time portfolio visibility, and more efficient program management in complex retail technology ecosystems.

Title: Building a Unified DOCSIS Platform with Go: Simplifying Multi-Vendor Deployment and Network Performance
Abstract: As broadband operators modernize DOCSIS networks, multi-vendor deployments introduce operational and engineering complexity. Differences in chipset behavior across vendors can impact channel bonding, modulation handling, error correction, telemetry, and overall service reliability. For teams responsible for delivering consistent performance at scale, reducing that variability is critical.  This talk explores how a unified DOCSIS chipset strategy, supported by Go-based platform engineering, can simplify deployment, improve interoperability, and create more predictable network operations. It will show how Go can be used to build high-performance services and tooling that normalize vendor-specific behavior, automate provisioning workflows, standardize firmware validation, and improve visibility across lab, trial, and production environments.  The session will cover the technical foundations behind unifying chipset operations across devices, including upstream and downstream processing, dynamic channel management, diagnostic collection, centralized monitoring, and rollout automation. It will also examine how Go’s concurrency model and performance characteristics make it well suited for network orchestration, telemetry pipelines, and large-scale operational tooling in cable environments.  Attendees will leave with practical insights into designing a more consistent DOCSIS platform across vendors, reducing troubleshooting overhead, accelerating field readiness, and preparing broadband infrastructure for high-split architectures and next-generation DOCSIS evolution.

Title: Designing Resilient Self-Service Search in Go: From Failure Modes to Intelligent Fallback Architectures
Abstract: Self-service search is often the first line of customer support—but also one of the most failure-prone systems. Traffic spikes, partial shard failures, or cascading fan-out effects can silently degrade results, leading to empty responses and increased support escalations.  This talk explores how to design a resilient search architecture in Go that guarantees meaningful results—even under failure conditions. Instead of treating fallback as an afterthought, we elevate it to a first-class reliability mechanism through a dedicated fallback index and intelligent routing strategies.  You’ll learn how fan-out amplification can turn rare outliers into systemic failures, and how a layered fallback approach mitigates this risk. We’ll walk through a production-ready architecture, including distributed retrieval, a lightweight fallback index optimized for efficiency, and circuit breaker–driven traffic routing (closed, open, half-open) for graceful degradation.  We’ll also cover bulkhead-based resource isolation to protect fallback capacity, practical strategies for avoiding zero-result queries, and how to balance recall and precision using query-log-driven synonym optimization.  Finally, we’ll connect infrastructure signals—like tail latency and circuit breaker activity—with user-centric metrics such as reformulation and escalation rates, enabling early detection of relevance issues before they impact users at scale.  Attendees will leave with actionable patterns and design principles to build robust, user-centric search systems that fail gracefully without compromising experience.

JobTitle1: Senior Software Developer and Team Lead
Title: Engineering High-Performance Real-Time Leaderboards: From O(N²) to O(log N)
Abstract: In this technical session, Ivan Balashov explores how to design and implement high-performance leaderboards capable of handling 100K+ concurrent users with real-time updates. He presents a case study where a production system suffered from daily outages due to O(N²) update complexity, CPU spikes, and memory pressure. The talk covers the end-to-end solution: an indexed skip list augmented with span counters for O(log N) position queries, combined with a hash-based user deduplication system. Ivan shares practical lessons from replacing a naive slice-based leaderboard with a memory-efficient, concurrency-safe, in-process solution delivered in just 2 days. Attendees will gain insight into probabilistic data structures, cache-aware design, and how to pragmatically balance technical excellence with urgent business requirements in production-critical systems.

Title: Designing for Defensibility: Evidence-Centric Compliance Architecture in Customer-Facing Financial Platforms
Abstract: Customer-facing financial web platforms operate under persistent regulatory scrutiny, continuous audit obligations, and a constant expectation of dispute-readiness. In these environments, compliance cannot be deferred to documentation or treated as a post-implementation concern — it must be deliberately engineered into the platform architecture from day one. This session introduces an evidence-centric compliance architecture approach in which regulatory obligations are translated into explicit architectural constraints that shape interaction flows, system boundaries, and operational controls from the ground up. The presentation examines how platforms can be designed to consistently produce structured, verifiable evidence of critical customer interactions — capturing what information was presented, how acknowledgement or consent was obtained, and how system decisions were derived. By embedding evidence generation directly into interaction workflows, platforms remain fully explainable during audits, regulatory inquiries, or disputes — without depending on manual reconstruction or assumptions after the fact. The session further explores auditability and traceability as first-class architectural qualities. Auditability enables confident, reliable reconstruction of critical events; traceability establishes clear links between regulatory expectations, specific design controls, and observable system behaviour. Together, these capabilities reduce interpretive ambiguity and support predictable compliance outcomes as platforms scale and evolve. The talk concludes by addressing governance and change-control practices that preserve compliance integrity over time. By identifying compliance-critical components early and applying risk-based review and release strategies, teams can modernise and innovate without eroding existing controls.

Title: Governance-Driven PAM Adoption for Hybrid and Cloud-Native Systems
Abstract: Privileged Access Management (PAM) is a critical control for securing administrative credentials and reducing high-risk access pathways in modern systems. This session presents a governance-driven approach to PAM adoption, emphasizing structured onboarding, identity lifecycle alignment, and integration with identity governance platforms such as SailPoint IdentityIQ.  The approach is delivered in two phases. The first secures Active Directory service and administrative accounts, covering both new and existing privileged identities. The second extends governance to cloud-based privileged accounts in Entra ID, ensuring consistent control across hybrid and cloud-native environments.  A key foundation is the discovery and classification of privileged accounts across systems, enabling prioritization based on criticality, account type, and operational context. This supports enforcement of least privilege principles and automated password rotation. Integration with identity governance ensures privileged access aligns with joiner, mover, and leaver processes through approval workflows, ownership metadata, and certification campaigns.  The session also addresses practical challenges such as connector reliability, password rotation timing, and user adoption of new workflows. These are mitigated through phased rollouts, configuration tuning, and targeted operational training.

Title: From Legacy to Go: Rebuilding Messaging Infrastructure Without Breaking Delivery
Abstract:
In fast-moving products, messaging infrastructure becomes a hidden weak point long before teams notice it.

In this talk, Pavel Zavialov shares how to migrate a legacy push and SMS platform to Go in a production environment spanning AWS, Docker, RabbitMQ, MongoDB, APNS, and GCM.

The talk focuses on the engineering decisions that made the migration safe: reducing operational fragility, defining cleaner service boundaries, handling asynchronous delivery flows, and improving reliability without slowing product teams down.

The audience will receive practical lessons on when Go is the right tool for infrastructure-heavy backend services, how to approach migration under load, and how to modernize distributed messaging systems effectively.

Title: Building High-Performance Predictive Analytics Services in Go for Education Finance
Abstract: Predictive analytics systems in education finance operate under strict performance, reliability, and scalability requirements. As institutions increasingly rely on real-time forecasts and risk signals to guide financial and academic decisions, the services powering these models must be efficient, concurrent, and production-ready. Go has emerged as a strong foundation for building such systems.  This session explores how Go is used to build high-performance predictive analytics services that support financial sustainability and student success. Drawing from real-world implementations, the talk examines how financial and student performance data is processed through Go-based APIs, streaming pipelines, and background workers to deliver timely, reliable predictions at scale.  A core focus is the design of Go services for risk assessment and resource allocation workflows. The presentation covers concurrency patterns, data ingestion strategies, and service orchestration techniques used to integrate predictive models with finance operations such as loan processing, grant disbursement, and compliance tracking. Emphasis is placed on writing maintainable, observable, and fault-tolerant Go services that operate in distributed environments.  The session also discusses how Go services interface with machine learning models through batch inference, real-time scoring, and asynchronous pipelines, balancing throughput, latency, and cost. Automation and AI-enabled workflows reduce manual oversight while improving accuracy and system scalability.  Looking ahead, the talk demonstrates how Go-based predictive platforms enable a shift from reactive reporting to proactive, data-driven decision systems. Attendees will gain practical insights into applying Go’s strengths simplicity, concurrency, and performance to build resilient analytics services that align financial sustainability with educational equity at scale.

Title: Automation in Go: Scaling DevOps, SRE, and Platform Engineering
Abstract: Elite software teams deploy 973x more frequently and recover from incidents 6570x faster than low performers (2024 DORA Report). At the heart of this performance gap lies automation. In Go-powered environments—where simplicity, concurrency, and performance are first-class—automation becomes a force multiplier across DevOps, SRE, and Platform Engineering.  This session explores how Go enables high-velocity delivery and resilient operations through automation at scale. We’ll examine intelligent CI/CD pipelines built with declarative patterns, policy-driven deployment gates, and ML-informed test selection that reduce lead times by over 80% and change failure rates by 65% (CD Foundation 2024). You’ll see how Infrastructure-as-Code, combined with Go-based tooling and AIOps principles, drives self-healing systems and reduces MTTR by up to 70%, while chaos engineering strengthens systemic resilience.  We’ll also demystify Internal Developer Platforms (IDPs) built with Go, where composable golden paths and self-service APIs abstract infrastructure complexity—accelerating onboarding by 45% and increasing deployment frequency by 2.5x (State of Internal Developer Portals 2024). Finally, we’ll explore SRE-as-Code and automated error budget enforcement to operationalize reliability and sustain 99.99% uptime SLAs.  Through real-world enterprise examples, this talk provides practical strategies for leveraging Go to scale automation maturity, improve delivery velocity, and build adaptive, resilient engineering organizations.

Title: Building AI-Driven Regulatory Compliance Systems with Go: Automating Legal Enforcement at Scale
Abstract: As regulatory requirements across finance, digital platforms, and global data protection frameworks grow increasingly complex, traditional compliance processes struggle to keep pace with real-time data and distributed systems. Modern organizations are turning to AI-driven solutions to automate monitoring, risk detection, and regulatory enforcement.  This session explores how AI-powered compliance platforms can be built using Go (Golang) to enable scalable and real-time regulatory monitoring. It discusses how Machine Learning (ML), Natural Language Processing (NLP), and predictive analytics can analyze operational data streams and identify compliance risks before they escalate.  From an engineering perspective, the talk highlights how Go’s concurrency model, performance, and microservices capabilities support the development of automated auditing services, real-time monitoring pipelines, risk scoring engines, and compliance reporting APIs within cloud-native architectures.  Through examples from content moderation, financial compliance (AML and fraud detection), and data protection governance, the session also addresses challenges such as legacy system integration, cross-jurisdictional regulations, and data governance.  Attendees will gain practical insights into designing scalable AI-driven compliance systems with Go, enabling organizations to automate regulatory enforcement while maintaining transparency and operational efficiency.

Title: Go-Powered Predictive Automation for Financial Approvals and Vendor Risk
Abstract: Enterprises are under growing pressure to optimize financial workflows, reduce operational costs, and improve decision-making accuracy. This talk explores how Go-based AI systems can power predictive automation for financial approval processes, with a focus on invoice management and vendor risk assessment. By building machine learning pipelines in Go and integrating models trained on historical vendor data, organizations can reduce invoice processing costs by up to 80%, lowering the average cost per invoice from $25 to as little as $5-15. These systems expedite approval timelines by 73% and improve invoice classification accuracy to 95-98%, compared to the 80-85% typical of traditional OCR approaches. Go's concurrency model and performance characteristics make it well suited for high-throughput financial automation. This session covers how Go services can reduce exception handling by 62% and improve fraud detection by 43%, enabling faster, more accurate decisions with significantly fewer manual interventions. Risk-based processing logic written in Go allows low-risk invoices to be auto-approved while high-risk submissions are flagged for review. The talk examines the technical foundations of these systems, including Go service architecture, data preprocessing pipelines, and enterprise integration patterns. It also covers model integration strategies for XGBoost and LightGBM, which deliver a 24% improvement in classification accuracy over baseline approaches. Attendees will leave with practical guidance on building Go-based AI pipelines that improve operational efficiency, strengthen compliance, and support strategic financial decision-making.

Title: AI-Driven Rate Limiting for Scalable Go-Based Cloud APIs
Abstract: Rate limiting is essential for protecting APIs, yet 73.4% of systems still rely on static thresholds—causing false positives in 41.8% of legitimate traffic bursts and significant revenue loss. In modern Go-based microservices handling highly dynamic workloads, these limitations become even more pronounced.  This session presents a practical framework for AI-powered rate limiting designed for cloud-native architectures built with Go. Instead of fixed rules, machine learning models analyze traffic across 27 behavioral features to distinguish legitimate high-volume usage from malicious activity. Studies show these approaches achieve up to 96.8% accuracy and reduce false positives by 68.7%.  We’ll walk through the full lifecycle: collecting 14+ traffic attributes, feature engineering, and selecting efficient models such as decision tree ensembles (up to 97.5% accuracy). The talk also covers implementation patterns for Go services and deployment across AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud.  Real-world results demonstrate systems that adapt to traffic spikes of up to 740% during peak events, block multiple DDoS vectors, and reduce infrastructure costs by 27% through smarter throttling. We’ll also explore progressive throttling, user segmentation, and continuous learning strategies that cut false positive impact by 87.3%.  Attendees will gain a clear, production-ready roadmap to implement intelligent, cost-efficient rate limiting in Go-based systems—enhancing security while maintaining performance and reliability.

JobTitle1: Manager - Software QA Engineering at Citizens
Title: AI-Assisted Test Execution for Scalable Go Quality Engineering
Abstract: AI-assisted test execution marks a shift from deterministic, script-driven automation toward adaptive quality engineering systems designed for complex enterprise environments. While regression testing research often validates techniques on small or mid-sized systems, evidence at true enterprise scale remains limited. This session presents AI-assisted test execution as an augmentation layer that strengthens existing Go-based testing frameworks rather than replacing them.  AI test agents combine perception capabilities, learning-based decision engines, and context-aware execution to interpret natural language requirements, navigate evolving interfaces, and validate system behavior under changing conditions. Empirical evaluations across enterprise web systems show that context-driven identification techniques remain resilient even when application structure, attributes, or content change conditions that commonly break traditional automation.  Unlike deterministic automation common in many Go testing pipelines, AI-driven systems operate probabilistically. This requires defined accuracy thresholds, governance controls, and structured human oversight. Research on autonomous systems testing shows that many approaches lack rigorous quantitative validation, making explainability, confidence-based escalation, and fallback strategies essential for enterprise adoption—particularly in regulated environments demanding traceability and auditability.  The session also explores how adaptive execution and learning-based defect prediction can reduce maintenance overhead while improving test effectiveness. Attendees will gain a research-grounded, practical framework for responsibly introducing AI-assisted execution into modern Go-centric engineering ecosystems balancing efficiency, scalability, and trust.

Title: From Code Correctness to Behavior Validation: Detecting Defects Early in Embedded Automotive Software
Abstract: In embedded automotive software, implementation correctness is only part of the reliability challenge. Control logic may be correctly written, compiled, and unit-tested, yet still fail at the system level when real-world behavioral expectations, integration assumptions, and cross-module interactions are not fully validated early in development.  As chassis and powertrain platforms become increasingly software-defined and interconnected, late-stage defects are more often caused by behavioral mismatches than by coding errors alone. These issues typically surface during system integration or vehicle-level testing, where diagnosis and correction are far more costly.  This session introduces a behavior-driven validation approach that shifts validation earlier in the development lifecycle. By explicitly defining expected system behavior and continuously validating it throughout design and implementation, teams can identify defects sooner, improve traceability between intent and execution, and reduce the risk of late-stage surprises.  Drawing from safety-critical automotive software contexts, this talk will show how behavior-driven validation frameworks help engineering teams detect integration defects earlier, align actual system behavior with design intent, and build greater confidence in complex embedded software systems.

JobTitle1: Global Head for AI / GenAI Products
Title: Building Cognitive Commerce Platforms in Go: Architecting LLM-Driven Intelligence for Grocery Retail and E-Commerce
Abstract: Grocery retail and e-commerce are evolving from rule-based automation toward cognitive, intent-aware systems powered by Large Language Models (LLMs). For engineering teams, this shift is not merely about model integration—it requires rethinking backend architectures, orchestration patterns, and real-time data pipelines. This talk explores how Go-based systems can serve as the backbone for production-grade LLM platforms in high-scale retail environments. We will examine architectural patterns for integrating LLMs into existing commerce stacks, including API gateways, streaming pipelines, concurrency-safe services, vector search infrastructure, and multimodal processing layers. Using practical retail scenarios, the session demonstrates how Go’s strengths—efficient concurrency, low-latency performance, and strong tooling—support scalable conversational commerce, personalized recommendations, and intelligent product discovery. On the merchandising side, we discuss building services that leverage LLMs for automated taxonomy generation, attribute extraction, and catalog enrichment across large, fast-changing assortments. Operationally, we explore LLM-enabled demand sensing, substitution intelligence, and fulfillment orchestration—highlighting how Go microservices coordinate real-time signals from online behavior, point-of-sale systems, and logistics workflows. The talk also covers agentic architectures: orchestrating LLM agents with Go-based services for controlled autonomy in warehouse management, micro-fulfillment, and last-mile delivery—while maintaining observability, reliability, and cost governance. Attendees will leave with concrete architectural patterns, implementation considerations, and production lessons for building resilient, scalable cognitive commerce platforms in Go that move beyond automation toward fully integrated, multimodal intelligence.

Title: Building an MCP Server in Go for a Real B2B Workflow
Abstract:
In this talk, I’ll show how I built an MCP server in Go for a real B2B workflow used inside a platform serving more than 1 million company profiles.

I’ll walk through how the server exposes a set of tools for Claude, connects to a GraphQL backend through AWS AppSync, and handles authentication safely in a production-oriented setup.

The session covers practical implementation details such as tool design, read/write boundaries, mutation safety, and how to structure integrations so LLM-driven workflows remain reliable instead of becoming fragile demos.

I’ll also explain how I approached testing with TDD, including mock GraphQL clients and API-level validation, so the MCP layer can evolve without breaking core behavior.

Attendees will receive a concrete path for building Go-based MCP services that connect real business systems, along with lessons on GraphQL integration, AWS authentication, and production-friendly testing patterns.

Title: Building High-Performance Workload Mobility in Go: Live Migration for GPU-Intensive Systems
Abstract: Modern AI/ML platforms rely on large-scale distributed systems to keep GPU-backed workloads running reliably, but failures are frequent and restarts are costly. At the core of enabling live workload migration at this scale is a high-performance control plane built in Go.  This talk dives into how Go is used to power live migration systems behind vMotion, enabling reliable mobility for GPU-intensive workloads in production. Drawing from real-world engineering experience, we’ll explore how Go’s concurrency model, channels, and lightweight goroutines are leveraged to orchestrate migrations, manage state transfer, and optimize throughput while keeping latency and compute-hour costs low.  We’ll cover practical challenges such as coordinating thousands of concurrent migrations, handling partial failures, implementing backpressure, and building robust error-handling and retry mechanisms. The session also highlights observability patterns in Go metrics, tracing, and structured logging that provide deep visibility into migration performance and reliability at scale.  Attendees will gain concrete insights into designing and operating large-scale, Go-based distributed systems that prioritize performance, resiliency, and correctness especially for stateful, GPU-heavy workloads.

Title: Profitability at Scale: Tesla vs GM Through a Data-Driven Lens
Abstract: The U.S. automotive industry offers a compelling case study in how contrasting business strategies translate into measurable financial outcomes. This talk presents a comparative analysis of the profitability and financial performance of Tesla Inc. and General Motors Company using published financial statement data over an extended period.  Key profitability metrics—including gross profit, operating profit, and net profit ratios—are examined to evaluate efficiency, financial resilience, and long-term sustainability. The analysis demonstrates that Tesla consistently achieves stronger gross profitability, reflecting its innovation-centric model and premium positioning. However, its operating and net profitability exhibit notable volatility, driven by heavy investment in research, development, and production expansion during periods of rapid growth.  In contrast, General Motors shows greater consistency in operating and net profitability, supported by large-scale manufacturing, a diversified product portfolio, and a mature market presence. Statistical testing confirms that while differences in gross profitability between the two firms are significant, variations in operating and net profitability are not statistically distinct.  By framing financial performance as a system shaped by strategic trade-offs—innovation-led growth versus scale-driven stability—this session offers engineers and technologists a structured, data-driven perspective on how complex organizations optimize performance over time. The talk provides practical insight for professionals interested in analytical thinking, performance measurement, and evidence-based decision-making in evolving industries.

Title: Scalable Design Systems for High-Performance Analytics Platforms
Abstract: Enterprise analytics platforms face increasing demands for consistency, accessibility, and performance across distributed teams and diverse interfaces. This session explores the architectural foundations required to build scalable design systems that remain reliable in complex, high-throughput environments often powered by modern backend services.  We focus on five measurable pillars of success: component modularity, token-driven theming, cross-platform consistency, versioning discipline, and governance maturity.  We begin with component modularity, showing how decoupled UI primitives reduce duplication, improve maintainability, and support parallel development across teams. Next, we examine token-based theming, where design tokens act as a single source of truth for visual and interaction properties, enabling consistent updates across multiple analytics surfaces.  We then address cross-platform consistency, outlining architectural patterns that ensure coherence across dashboards, embedded analytics, and operational tools while accommodating product-level variations. The session also covers versioning strategies, including semantic versioning and controlled release channels, to evolve systems without breaking dependent applications.  Finally, we explore governance models that enable sustainable scaling, including contribution workflows, review standards, accessibility enforcement, and decision frameworks that balance autonomy with alignment.

Title: NLB Generative Frameworks for Scalable E2E Testing in Go
Abstract: Modern software validation is in crisis. Traditional automation frameworks built on hardcoded locators and deterministic scripts consume up to 70% of QA engineering time in pure maintenance, stifling innovation and slowing deployment velocity. This talk covers Autonomous Cognitive Navigation (ACN), a Natural Language Based (NLB) generative framework built in Go that replaces brittle, script-driven testing with intent-based reasoning. At its core is a State-Aware Reasoning Loop powered by a Large Language Model that interprets real-time UI state, maintains a full chronological action history, and resolves high-entropy flows spanning 20+ steps, all from a single natural language instruction. Three concrete technical contributions drive the framework's performance: a Persistent Temporal Action Memory that eliminates infinite-loop failures common in prior embedding-based AI systems; a Semantic Canonicalization function that reduces raw UI view hierarchy token volume by 85% for faster reasoning; and a multimodal end-state verification engine that evaluates success visually, making it resilient to UI redesigns that break identifier-based checks. Attendees will hear what was built, what went wrong, what worked, and what changed along the way. ACN achieves a 95% reliability rate across thousands of localized application variants and 70+ languages. Onboarding new test flows drops from 120 hours of manual scripting to under 4 hours of natural language definition, translating to an estimated 27 developer-years of saved maintenance effort and a 15-20% increase in feature release velocity. Relevant to Go engineers, QA practitioners, and engineering leaders navigating the scalability demands of modern CI/CD pipelines.

Title: AI-Driven Fraud Detection & Personalization in Scalable Go Fintech Systems
Abstract: The rapid growth of mobile fintech applications has reshaped digital banking, increasing both transaction volumes and exposure to sophisticated fraud such as account takeovers, synthetic identities, and money laundering. At the same time, users expect highly personalized financial experiences. This session presents a unified AI-driven approach to fraud prevention and personalization, tailored for high-performance systems built with Go.  We explore modern machine learning architectures—including gradient-boosted decision trees, RNNs, graph neural networks, and transformer-based models—for real-time anomaly detection across high-frequency transaction streams. Special focus is given to how Go’s concurrency model, low-latency execution, and efficient memory management enable scalable inference pipelines and streaming architectures. Key performance metrics such as detection accuracy, false-positive rates, and inference latency are examined, highlighting trade-offs in production fintech systems.  On the personalization side, we cover recommendation systems, reinforcement learning, and LLM-powered conversational agents that enable adaptive credit scoring, behavior-aware insights, and tailored product offerings. These systems are evaluated based on relevance, engagement, and user trust.  We also address real-world challenges including class imbalance, concept drift, explainability under regulations (GDPR, PSD2, EU AI Act), and fairness in lending. Privacy-preserving techniques such as federated learning, differential privacy, and on-device inference are discussed in the context of secure Go-based deployments.  Through industry case studies, attendees will see how AI systems improve fraud detection while enhancing customer experience. We conclude with emerging challenges in adversarial robustness, real-time graph analytics, and responsible AI adoption in financial systems.

JobTitle1: Indian Institute of Engineering Science and Technology
Company1: Indian Institute of Engineering Science and Technology
Title: Autonomous Cloud Infrastructure with Go: Building Self-Healing Systems
Abstract: Enterprises are losing an average of dollars per minute due to unplanned IT downtime (Gartner), highlighting the urgent need for resilient, self-healing infrastructure. At Conf42 Golang 2026, this session explores how Go-powered cloud platforms are enabling the rise of the autonomous enterprise through intelligent, self-managing systems.  We’ll examine how AIOps adoption has accelerated in recent years, allowing teams to automate incident detection, root-cause analysis, and remediation using machine learning and predictive analytics. With real-time anomaly detection across logs and metrics, AI-driven models are helping reduce mean time to detect and mean time to resolve, strengthening reliability in distributed Go-based microservices environments.  The talk will unpack practical use cases where predictive failure prevention minimized outages and how policy-driven orchestration frameworks evolved from simple provisioning to closed-loop automation—forming the backbone of resilient cloud-native systems. We’ll also explore the next phase of Infrastructure-as-Code, where intelligent configurations dynamically adapt to operational context.  Through platforms such as Azure Automanage, AWS Fault Injection Simulator, and GCP Active Assist, organizations are automating remediation and chaos validation, enabling engineering teams to focus on innovation.  Attendees will gain practical architectural patterns, tooling strategies, and cultural insights needed to design and operate autonomous, self-healing cloud systems using Go.

Title: Building Cloud-Native Insurance Core Systems with Go
Abstract: Insurance organizations are under mounting pressure to modernize legacy core platforms as customer expectations rise, regulatory demands increase, and digital-native competitors accelerate innovation. Decades-old monolithic systems built for stability now limit agility, require lengthy release cycles, and struggle to handle demand surges such as catastrophe-driven claims spikes.  This session examines how cloud-native engineering—implemented with Go-based microservices—enables resilient, high-velocity core systems. We explore service decomposition, containerization, and Kubernetes orchestration to support independent scaling of high-volume capabilities like quote generation and claims intake. Kubernetes enhances availability through automated deployment, scaling, self-healing, and progressive delivery strategies including canary and blue-green releases.  Infrastructure-as-code improves repeatability and disaster recovery by enabling complete environment reconstruction within hours rather than weeks. CI/CD automation compresses release cycles from quarterly deployments to multiple releases per day, supported by automated functional, performance, and security testing. Event-driven architectures using platforms such as Kafka or EventBridge increase resilience by reducing cascading failures and supporting eventual consistency. API-first design enables secure partner integrations through documented interfaces, centralized authentication, and versioned contracts.  Modern observability—centralized logging, distributed tracing, and metrics—provides visibility across ecosystems of distributed services. The session also addresses challenges including distributed data consistency, operational complexity, and cultural change, outlining practical solutions grounded in the research.  Attendees will gain a research-backed roadmap for transforming legacy insurance platforms into agile, scalable cloud-native systems using Go.

Title: Beyond Chatbots: Building Actionable AI with Scalable Backend Integrations in Go
Abstract: AI chatbots have become ubiquitous in customer-facing applications, yet many fail to deliver meaningful outcomes. The issue isn’t the language model—it’s the lack of deep backend integration. Without real-time connectivity to systems like CRM, order management, and billing platforms, chatbots remain conversational interfaces that cannot execute actions, leading to poor resolution rates and increased escalations.  This talk explores how to transform chatbots from passive responders into fully actionable systems using robust backend architectures. Focusing on Go-based services, we’ll dive into integration patterns that enable bots to perform end-to-end operations such as order cancellations, delivery tracking, and billing resolution.  You’ll learn practical approaches including API orchestration, event-driven design, caching strategies, and real-time data pipelines that ensure performance at scale. We’ll also cover security considerations for handling sensitive data in automated workflows.  Through real-world examples from e-commerce and service platforms, this session provides a blueprint for building AI systems that don’t just converse—but act.

JobTitle1: Senior Oracle Cloud PPM - Solution Architect
Title: From Reactive to Predictive: Building an AI-Embedded Project Financial Governance Framework with Go
Abstract: Traditional Oracle Cloud PPM environments operate reactively—cost overruns, revenue leakage, and compliance violations are typically discovered only after financial impact has already occurred. This session introduces a production-grade, AI-embedded predictive governance framework engineered using Go to transform project financial management from retrospective reporting into anticipatory, real-time control.  At the core of the framework is a set of high-performance, concurrent Go microservices that embed machine learning inference directly into project financial workflows without requiring modifications to Oracle Cloud. By leveraging Go’s strengths in concurrency, lightweight goroutines, efficient memory management, and scalable service orchestration, the architecture enables real-time transaction validation, predictive burn-rate analysis, intelligent revenue recognition monitoring, and forward-looking portfolio risk assessment across complex project environments.  The solution integrates five intelligent layers working together within an event-driven architecture. An Allowability Intelligence Engine evaluates transactions before posting to prevent non-compliant costs. A Forecast Drift Learning Engine continuously improves forecast accuracy through historical behavioral pattern modeling. Funding-to-Forecast Intelligence predicts burn-rate risk across multi-award portfolios. A Revenue Leakage Sentinel performs streaming transaction analysis to detect missed billing opportunities in real time. Finally, a Decision Intelligence layer provides scenario modeling and predictive portfolio simulations for executive planning.  Built with Go-based APIs, concurrent validation pipelines, and scalable inference services, this framework supports high-throughput financial ecosystems operating under federal grant regulations, ASC 606 compliance requirements, and complex contract structures. Implementation across large-scale portfolios demonstrates measurable improvements in billing acceleration, revenue capture, forecast accuracy, financial close efficiency, and proactive compliance management. Most importantly, the architectural patterns are portable and vendor-agnostic, enabling organizations to embed AI-driven governance capabilities without invasive ERP customization.

Title: Modularizing a 10-Year Monolith: The Architecture, The People, and the Pain
Abstract: Most Go codebases begin as straightforward layered monoliths, but years of growth often turn those layers into a web of hidden coupling, unclear ownership, and hard-to-predict side effects. Small changes start requiring deep context, and compile and test times slowly creep up, turning routine work into risky work. Rewrites promise a clean slate but rarely succeed in practice. What the Go community lacks are real examples of large open source Go projects that have successfully evolved toward a modular monolith.  This talk presents a major open source Go project in the middle of that evolution. It covers how we are moving from a decade-old layered architecture toward a modular design while continuing to ship features to multiple production environments that teams actively depend on every day. This is not theory. This is architectural change in a live system, with real contributors, long CI pipelines, social constraints, and legacy assumptions embedded throughout the code.  What began as an investigation into slow build and test times exposed deeper problems: oversized packages, uncontrolled dependencies, unclear boundaries, and an architecture that no longer matched how engineers actually reasoned about the system. We walk through familiar pain points in large Go monoliths, including tight coupling, long feedback cycles, and frequent merge conflicts, and explain why these problems persist even in well-intentioned codebases. You’ll see where our early attempts stalled, how architectural changes regressed quietly over time, why good ideas alone were not enough, and how steady, incremental refactoring helped us regain control without freezing development. Architecture only works when people agree to carry it together.  Whether you are working on a fast-growing Go project or maintaining a mature production system, this talk will give you concrete techniques and mental models for evolving your architecture safely. You’ll leave with a clearer understanding of how to introduce boundaries that hold, align ownership with code, and make a large system feel smaller, safer, and easier to change, so teams can move faster without needing to hold the entire system in their heads.

Title: Designing Data Center Networks for Distributed ML Training Workloads
Abstract: The rapid growth of machine learning training workloads is pushing cloud data center networks beyond traditional design assumptions. Unlike loosely coupled applications dominated by north-south traffic, distributed training systems depend on tightly synchronized communication across large clusters, generating sustained, high-volume east-west traffic. Each iteration requires timely completion of collective operations such as all-reduce, making network bandwidth, latency consistency, and congestion behavior critical to overall training efficiency.  This session explores how these workload characteristics reshape modern network architecture. It examines communication patterns in distributed training, where gradient exchanges can involve gigabytes of data per iteration and require simultaneous, bidirectional communication across hundreds or thousands of nodes. Traditional oversubscribed designs struggle under these conditions, while architectures such as low-oversubscription leaf-spine fabrics, multi-rail networks, and RDMA-enabled transport better support the required throughput and latency.  Using real-world observations, the talk highlights how synchronization overhead grows with cluster size, how congestion propagates across tightly coupled systems, and how even small latency variations can stall training progress. It also discusses emerging approaches such as in-network aggregation and programmable fabrics that aim to mitigate these challenges.  Attendees will gain practical insights into designing networks for large-scale ML systems and understanding the trade-offs that impact performance, efficiency, and cost in modern cloud environments.

Title: Scalable Omni-Channel Order Systems with Go Microservices
Abstract: The U.S. retail sector is rapidly evolving toward unified, frictionless customer experiences across online, mobile, and in-store channels. At the core of this transformation are Omni-Channel Order Management Systems that synchronize inventory, fulfillment, and customer data across distributed retail networks.  Modern architectures are shifting from legacy ERP-centered monoliths to microservices and event-driven systems capable of real-time coordination. In Go-centric cloud-native environments, lightweight concurrency and efficient service communication patterns support low-latency order orchestration and improved fulfillment accuracy. Research indicates that such distributed architectures enhance operational responsiveness and system reliability in large-scale retail deployments.  AI-assisted decision systems further strengthen predictive demand planning and adaptive inventory allocation, improving supply chain efficiency through analytics-driven orchestration. Elastic cloud scaling and edge-aware fulfillment models also contribute to operational sustainability by optimizing compute consumption and reducing energy-intensive overprovisioning.  Drawing from large-scale retail transformation case studies, this session presents a practical reference framework for designing scalable, resilient, and sustainable omni-channel order management platforms. Key architectural enablers include real-time data integration, event choreography, dynamic inventory intelligence, and observability patterns essential for building next-generation retail systems using cloud-native microservices.

Title: Data-Driven Warehouse Design for Scalable, Risk-Resilient 3PL RFPs
Abstract: Large-scale 3PL RFPs demand more than competitive pricing—they require data-backed operational credibility. Yet many proposals still rely on generic assumptions, creating misalignment with real-world operations and increasing implementation risk. This session presents a data-driven warehouse solution design framework that transforms raw logistics data into accurate, scalable, and implementation-ready proposals.  The approach begins with throughput analysis, using inbound metrics such as containers received, pallets handled, and peak variability, alongside outbound indicators like order volume, order complexity, and shipment patterns to define true capacity requirements. It then extends to order profile analysis, where SKU breadth, order size distribution, and pick types inform the selection of optimal fulfillment strategies such as batch, zone, or wave picking.  A key component is SKU and inventory profiling through velocity-based segmentation to optimize storage placement, reduce picker travel time, and improve throughput efficiency. These insights guide warehouse layout and storage design, aligning material flow, dock configuration, and storage systems with actual operational constraints.  The session also covers labor modeling, translating operational volumes into workforce requirements using engineering standards to ensure accurate planning in labor-intensive environments. Equipment and facility investments are derived directly from these models, enabling transparent and defensible cost structures. Finally, the framework emphasizes customer validation and data continuity, ensuring alignment before submission and a smooth transition from proposal to implementation.

Title: Building Self-Healing Real-Time Streaming Pipelines with Go and AI
Abstract: Modern streaming platforms demand autonomously managed pipelines that can adapt to fluctuating workloads and strict real-time constraints. This talk presents a self-healing, AI-native real-time data pipeline architecture tailored for Go-driven distributed systems. Designed for high-throughput environments processing telemetry, IoT streams, and multimodal event data, the architecture leverages Go’s lightweight concurrency model to build resilient, scalable streaming microservices.  We explore how AI-driven anomaly detection, predictive autoscaling, and automated fault recovery can be implemented using Go-based control loops and orchestration layers. A closed-loop reinforcement learning control plane dynamically adjusts ingestion, messaging, and compute tiers to mitigate disruptions such as node failures, network congestion, schema drift, and burst traffic. The session also covers integration patterns with streaming ecosystems like Kafka, Pulsar, and Flink, while maintaining strong consistency guarantees and exactly-once semantics.  Through real-world evaluation across diverse streaming workloads, we highlight measurable improvements in resilience, reduced recovery times, and stable throughput under burst conditions. Attendees will gain a practical, Go-centric blueprint for building autonomous, self-healing streaming infrastructures that combine cloud-native design with AI-driven operational intelligence—enabling resilient, adaptive data platforms for next-generation real-time systems.
